indications.rationale: Allergic rhinitis (paroxysmal sneezing, watery rhinorrhea, nasal congestion and itching) corresponds in Ayurveda to a Kapha-predominant, frequently Vata-Kapha, vitiation of the pranavaha srotas (the Vataja-Kaphaja pratishyaya pattern). Haridra (turmeric, Curcuma longa) is katu-tikta in rasa, ushna in virya, katu in vipaka, with laghu-ruksha guna, which makes it kaphaghna and shothahara: it dries and disperses the excess Kapha (mucus/secretions) and performs srotoshodhana, clearing and decongesting the respiratory channels. Its drying, channel-opening (tikshna/lekhana-leaning) action is the first-principles basis for easing the boggy, congested, hypersecretory nasal mucosa typical of this concern, consistent with its documented kapha-decreasing dosha effect. It is classically grouped among kaphaja/shvasa-pratishyaya-relevant drugs and appears in traditional respiratory and anti-allergic formulas.

indications.rationale: Ginger (Ārdraka/Śuṇṭhī) acts on nausea through its core dravyaguṇa profile. Its katu (pungent) rasa, ushna (heating) vīrya and laghu guṇa make it a classic dīpana-pāchana dravya that kindles agni and digests āma; in Ayurvedic terms occasional nausea (hṛllāsa / chardi pravṛtti) arises chiefly from mandāgni and the upward (ūrdhva) movement of vitiated Kapha-Vata in the āmāśaya. By warming and stimulating digestion, drying excess Kapha and pacifying Vata, ginger settles this upward urge and restores the normal downward (adho) flow — which is why a slice with rock salt, or fresh ginger tea before travel or meals, is the time-honoured remedy. Its madhura vipāka tempers the heat enough that it is unusually well tolerated for a pungent herb. This first-principles action — anti-emetic via agni-kindling and Kapha-Vata anulomana — is consistent with modern findings: ginger's gingerols and shogaols are best supported as promoting gastric emptying / prokinetic motility, with proposed (and still largely preclinical) 5-HT3 receptor antagonism as a contributing mechanism for nausea relief.

indications.rationale: Dravyaguna basis matches the verified herb record (rasa: tikta + kashaya; guna: laghu, ruksha; virya: shita/sheeta; vipaka: katu). This tikta-kashaya, sheeta-ruksha profile is well suited to the mukha/danta srotas. The tikta and kashaya rasas are lekhana (scraping) and stambhana, cutting kapha-driven sliminess on teeth and gums (kledanashana), firming boggy or bleeding gingiva, and reducing the sweet, sticky oral milieu that feeds dental kleda. Neem's classical krimighna (antimicrobial/vermicidal) karma targets the 'krimi' traditionally blamed for danta-roga and oral decay, corresponding to the microbial plaque biofilm. Its sheeta virya and pitta-shamaka quality calm the inflammatory rakta-pitta component of sore, swollen, bleeding gums, while the ruksha guna counters kleda on teeth and tongue and helps daurgandhya (malodor). Classical dinacharya places bitter-astringent twigs (Nimba among them) as danta-dhavana dravyas: chewing/brushing with the twig stimulates saliva and mechanically and chemically de-films the teeth. Modern pharmacology is consistent with this mechanism: neem leaf/twig extracts show antibacterial and anti-biofilm activity against cariogenic and periodontal organisms. Use is topical (mouthrinse / danta-dhavana), so the action is local and mechanistically coherent.

indications.rationale: Guggulu (purified oleo-gum resin of Commiphora wightii) is the archetypal lekhana (scraping) and medohara (fat-reducing) dravya of the classical materia medica, and weight_management here maps to the classical concept of medoroga/sthaulya. By dravyaguna first principles its action opposes the target directly: medoroga is understood as excess Meda dhatu driven by aggravated Kapha plus mandagni (sluggish metabolic fire), and guggulu's profile is precisely contrary - tikta-katu-kashaya rasa, ruksha (dry) and tikshna (sharp/penetrating) guna, and ushna virya. These dry, sharp, heating qualities counter the heavy, unctuous, cold qualities of Kapha and Meda (samanya-vishesha: opposites reduce), while its dipana-pachana tendency helps rekindle agni so that ama and excess fat are 'scraped' (lekhana) rather than accumulated. This is why guggulu is the named resin in the classical anti-obesity formulas - Triphala guggulu, Navaka guggulu and Medohar guggulu - used as an adjunct alongside diet (pathya) and activity, not as a standalone slimming agent. Honest evidence note: the human RCT evidence for guggulu specifically reducing body weight/BMI/waist is small, mixed, and mostly tests combination herbo-mineral formulas (often paired with diet), with at least one trial finding guggulu alone no better than diet; the better-replicated clinical effect is on serum lipids, not on weight. Support is therefore classical/traditional rather than clinically proven for weight loss - distinctly weaker than e.g. ginger for nausea - and it should be presented as lifestyle/medoroga support, not a weight-loss treatment.

indications.rationale: Cardamom (Elā) is katu-madhura in rasa with a laghu (light) guṇa, an unusually mild (śīta / mildly cooling) vīrya for an aromatic spice, and a madhura vipāka. By dravyaguṇa this makes it a gentle dīpana and aromatic carminative that can settle an upward-moving, queasy stomach (the urdhvaga / pratiloma vāta underlying hrillāsa) without the strong heat that would aggravate Pitta. Its volatile aromatics are mukha-daurgandhya-nāśana — they freshen the mouth and ease the queasy reflex — and because it pacifies Vata and Kapha while staying close to neutral for Pitta, it is one of the few carminatives tolerated across all three doshas, including where ginger's ushṇa vīrya may feel too heating. Honesty note: unlike ginger (Śuṇṭhī), for which controlled human trials support an antiemetic effect, cardamom's anti-nausea use rests on classical materia medica and carminative practice, not on robust RCTs — hence 'traditional' rather than 'preliminary'/'clinical'.

indications.rationale: In Ayurveda arsha (piles) is largely a disorder of vitiated apana vayu with mandagni and vibandha (constipation): hard stool and straining against a downward-blocked apana aggravate the rectal vessels. Haritaki (Terminalia chebula), named Abhaya, is the classic remedy for exactly this mechanism. As the premier anulomana/mridu-rechana it restores the natural downward flow of apana, softens and eases stool, and relieves the straining that provokes and worsens piles. Its kashaya (astringent) rasa is regarded as sangrahi/stambhana on local tissue, the dravyaguna basis claimed for reducing bleeding and the laxity of pile-masses, while its ushna virya and dipana action address the underlying mandagni so ama and stagnation are less likely to re-form. Being tridoshic and a rasayana, it targets the constipation root cause without the harsh purging that would further aggravate Vata. This is the rationale for its long classical use, alone and within Triphala, in the management of arsha. The benefit here is supportive (easing constipation and straining), not a treatment of the hemorrhoid itself.
indications.note: Comfort/regularity support for piles by easing constipation and straining; not a treatment for acute, thrombosed, or actively bleeding hemorrhoids. Honest tier is 'traditional': haritaki has strong, cross-lineage classical use for arsha but no credible standalone RCT for hemorrhoids, so 'clinical' would be unwarranted. The modern-research citation is a pointer to the laxative/stool-softening literature on Terminalia chebula / Triphala that is mechanistically relevant to straining; it is flagged requires_verification because no exact study reference is attached and the modern evidence is not hemorrhoid-specific. Avoid in pregnancy. Persistent rectal bleeding needs medical evaluation.

indications.rationale: Gokshura (Gokṣura, Tribulus terrestris) is classically classed as a balya (strengthening) and vṛṣya (reproductive/aphrodisiac tonic) dravya, which makes it a coherent fit for general vitality, stamina and reproductive-vigour wellness support. By dravyaguṇa first principles its supportive action follows from its properties: madhura rasa and madhura vipāka are bṛṃhaṇa (nourishing) and build the deeper dhātus including śukra; its guru-snigdha guṇa give a grounding, anabolic quality that counters depletion; and its predominantly cooling/Vāta-Pitta-pacifying nature lets it restore energy without the heating, drying aggravation of more pungent tonics. This is why classical materia medica places it among vṛṣya/balya herbs used for stamina and reproductive wellness, and why it suits people whose low vitality stems from Vāta depletion or Pitta-type heat. This is general energy/stamina and reproductive-vitality wellness support, not treatment of erectile dysfunction or infertility. (Note: Gokshura is also strongly classical as a mūtrala/diuretic for urinary health; that is a separate, arguably stronger classical indication and is not the basis of this vitality entry.)
indications.note: Honest tiering. Although Gokshura/Tribulus is widely marketed as a vitality and testosterone-boosting supplement, the modern human-trial evidence is weak and inconsistent: most controlled RCTs in healthy/young men show no significant rise in serum testosterone, and ergogenic/stamina ('vitality') endpoints are largely unsupported; only a few small, mixed trials suggest possible libido or sexual-function benefit (and some positive testosterone signals are confounded by proprietary multi-herb formulations rather than Tribulus alone). This does NOT meet a 'clinical' bar for the vitality/stamina indication, and even 'preliminary' would overstate the specifically positive evidence for stamina, so the tier rests on the strong classical balya/vṛṣya grounding and is set to 'traditional'. The modern_research citation is an uncited placeholder claim and MUST be replaced with a real, retrievable reference (journal/year/DOI) before publication.

safety: Educational wellness information only - not a diagnosis, treatment, or cure. Persistent or severe diarrhoea, blood or mucus in stool, high fever, signs of dehydration, or symptoms lasting more than 48 hours need prompt medical evaluation, not self-care; diarrhoea in infants and the elderly is especially serious.

Pregnancy: avoid medicinal/concentrated doses. Traditional sources and animal studies flag the fruit/leaf for possible reproductive effects (anti-fertility and uterine activity reported in animals), and the ushna virya is generally cautioned. Use only ripe fruit as an ordinary food unless a qualified practitioner advises otherwise.
Lactation: insufficient reliable safety data for medicinal doses during breastfeeding; restrict to culinary use of ripe fruit and consult a qualified practitioner before using churna or decoctions.

Fertility/reproductive caution: some animal studies report reduced male fertility/spermatogenesis and anti-implantation effects with leaf and seed extracts. Those actively trying to conceive (male or female) should use medicinal preparations only under professional guidance.

Blood sugar / antidiabetic interaction: Aegle marmelos has shown blood-glucose-lowering activity in preliminary animal and in-vitro studies (no robust human RCT data). It may add to the effect of antidiabetic drugs or insulin - monitor for hypoglycaemia (shakiness, sweating, confusion) and have a clinician review medication doses. Because of possible glucose-lowering effects, consider stopping medicinal use about 1-2 weeks before scheduled surgery.

Drug and nutrient absorption: the astringent, tannin-rich pulp may bind and reduce absorption of some oral medications and of minerals such as iron - separate Bilva from medicines and supplements by about 2 hours. A theoretical effect on thyroid function has been raised in animal data only; this is not an established human interaction, but those on thyroid medication should use caution and seek professional advice.

Constipating/overdose effects: the unripe fruit is strongly grahi/binding - excessive or prolonged use can cause constipation, abdominal heaviness, flatulence or aggravate vata. The unripe fruit is contraindicated where stools are already hard or in vata-type constipation. The ripe fruit, by contrast, is mildly laxative, so the two parts have OPPOSITE bowel effects and must not be confused.

Species/preparation confusion: 'Bael' (Aegle marmelos) must not be confused with 'wood apple' / kapittha (Limonia acidissima, syn. Feronia limonia), a different fruit, nor with questionable 'bilva patra' substitutions in commerce. Verify botanical identity (Aegle marmelos) on any product. Avoid the seeds and large quantities of excess unripe pulp.

Evidence honesty: modern pharmacological signals for Aegle marmelos (antidiarrhoeal, antimicrobial, hypoglycaemic, anti-fertility, thyroid effects) come from preliminary in-vitro/animal studies; robust human clinical (RCT) evidence is lacking. Do not treat these as proven benefits.

General caution: those with very high pitta or acute hyperacidity should use cautiously given the ushna virya. Discontinue and seek care if rash, significant GI upset, or unexpected drops in blood sugar occur. Keep within labeled dosage; if taking prescription medication or managing a chronic condition, consult a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner and your physician before use.

safety: Educational information only; not a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Acute or persistent diarrhea, blood in stool, high fever, dehydration, or severe abdominal pain require prompt medical care - do not self-treat with herbs.

Pregnancy: Avoid during pregnancy unless supervised by a qualified practitioner. The conessine-type steroidal alkaloids are pharmacologically active and safety in pregnancy is not established; conservative practice is to avoid.
Lactation: Insufficient reliable safety data during breastfeeding; avoid or use only under qualified professional supervision.

Drug interactions: May add to or interfere with antidiarrheal, antiparasitic/amoebicidal, antihypertensive, and CNS-active medications. Conessine and related steroidal alkaloids have reported hypotensive and central nervous system activity, so additive hypotension with antihypertensives and additive effects with sedatives/CNS drugs are theoretically possible. Use cautiously alongside any prescription drug and consult a clinician/pharmacist, especially with cardiac, antihypertensive, or CNS-active medicines.

Toxicity/overdose: The active alkaloid conessine is cardiotoxic and can cause central nervous system effects (reported vertigo, tremor, insomnia, agitation/anxiety) and hypotension at higher doses in pharmacological/animal studies. Do not exceed traditional/formulary doses; prolonged high-dose use is discouraged.

Constitutional caution: Its ruksha (drying) and shita qualities may aggravate Vata and can worsen constipation if overused; avoid in marked Vata depletion, severe dryness, or pre-existing constipation without guidance.

Species confusion/adulteration: Botanical identity matters - the accepted species is Holarrhena pubescens (synonym Holarrhena antidysenterica). 'Kutaja' bark is commonly confused or adulterated in trade with Wrightia tinctoria (Sweta Kutaja/Dudhi) and other Wrightia/Holarrhena species; Indrayava (Kutaja seed) is sometimes substituted with look-alike seeds. Although Wrightia tinctoria also contains some conessine, its alkaloid content and therapeutic properties differ, so it is not an equivalent substitute. Source from reputable suppliers with proper botanical authentication.
Pediatric and elderly use, and use in those with chronic disease, should be under qualified supervision.
Discontinue and seek care if symptoms worsen or do not improve within a couple of days, or if any allergic or adverse reaction occurs.

safety: Generally regarded as safe in traditional culinary and dietary amounts; for therapeutic use, stay within classical dosage ranges (~3-6 g powder/day) under a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner.

PREGNANCY - avoid therapeutic/medicinal doses (treat as contraindicated in pregnancy). Cyperus rotundus has documented traditional use as an emmenagogue and to influence the uterus/menstrual flow, and preliminary animal/in-vitro data suggest anti-implantation and estrogenic activity; human reproductive-safety is not established. (Reproductive-pharmacology signals are non-clinical and require verification.)

LACTATION - classically described as stanyashodhana (purifies/modifies breast milk); human safety data during breastfeeding are lacking, so medicinal-dose use while nursing should be avoided unless directed by a qualified practitioner.

Due to ruksha (drying) and grahi (stool-checking) qualities it can aggravate Vata and may worsen constipation, dryness, or pre-existing Vata conditions if overused; not suitable as a standalone for atonic/vata-type constipation.

BLOOD SUGAR / METABOLIC (theoretical) - non-clinical (in-vitro/animal) reports suggest possible glucose- and lipid-modulating effects; a theoretical additive effect with antidiabetic or lipid-lowering drugs cannot be excluded. Monitor and coordinate with a clinician if combining. Not confirmed in controlled human trials (requires verification).

DRUG INTERACTIONS (theoretical) - possible additive effects with sedative, anti-inflammatory, or gastrointestinal-acting medications are plausible only on the basis of preliminary pharmacology; robust human interaction data are lacking (requires verification).

SPECIES CONFUSION / ADULTERATION - 'Nagarmotha' and related trade names are applied to several Cyperus species (e.g., C. scariosus, C. esculentus, C. articulatus) and the rhizome trade is prone to substitution; raw material may also carry heavy-metal or microbial contamination. Source authenticated Cyperus rotundus from reputable suppliers with identity and contaminant (heavy-metal) testing.

OVERDOSE / TOXICITY - no well-characterized acute toxicity at traditional doses, but excessive intake may cause undue dryness, Vata aggravation, or GI upset; discontinue and seek advice if adverse effects occur. Children, the elderly, and those with debility should use lower doses under guidance.

Educational use only - this information does not diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease. Consult a qualified healthcare professional before use, especially if pregnant, breastfeeding, on medication, or managing a health condition.

It is classically a deepana-pachana (appetizer-digestive) drug supporting weak digestion and clearing ama-related sluggishness; this is one of its core, consistently described classical actions.

preparation: with honey or jaggery as anupana

taken with buttermilk (takra) or warm water in digestive use
typical_dosage: Churna (powder): 2-4 g per day in divided doses (commonly 1-3 g), taken with warm water, honey, or buttermilk; decoction (kwatha) 20-40 ml. Use under qualified guidance.
safety: Educational wellness information only — not a diagnosis, treatment, or cure for any condition. Classical 'krimighna' (anthelmintic) use is NOT a substitute for medical diagnosis or treatment of parasitic/worm infection; consult a qualified healthcare practitioner.
Pregnancy: Contraindicated. Embelin (the principal constituent) has documented antifertility and anti-implantation activity in animal studies; avoid during pregnancy.
Conception/fertility: Avoid when actively trying to conceive. Embelin shows reported antifertility and anti-spermatogenic effects in animal models, relevant to both partners.
Lactation: Insufficient human safety data; avoid during breastfeeding unless specifically directed by a qualified practitioner.

Pitta/heat conditions: Use cautiously or avoid in high pitta, hyperacidity, gastritis, peptic ulcer, or bleeding tendencies; its ushna-tikshna nature can aggravate pitta and cause heartburn, acidity, gastric irritation, nausea, or loose stools. Discontinue if such symptoms occur.
Dose/overdose: Stay within traditional dosing; do not exceed recommended amounts.

Drug interactions (theoretical/preliminary, based on animal pharmacology, not confirmed clinically): possible additive effects with antidiabetic agents (monitor blood sugar) and lipid-lowering agents; use caution with anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy. Inform your clinician and separate dosing if on prescription medication.
Children, elderly, and debilitated persons: use only under professional guidance at reduced dose.

Adulteration / species confusion: Genuine Vidanga is the fruit of Embelia ribes. Markets commonly substitute Embelia tsjeriam-cottam and, notably, Maesa indica ('false vidanga'), which differ in constituents; embelin content varies and adulterated material may be ineffective or of uncertain safety. Source authenticated, lab-verified material.
Quality/contaminants: As with all herbal powders, choose suppliers testing for heavy metals, microbial load, and correct botanical identity.

indications.rationale: Chitrak is the classical archetype of a dipana-pachana (digestive-fire kindling) dravya; its synonyms Agni and Vahni ('fire') reflect this core action. Katu rasa, ushna virya, laghu-tikshna guna and katu vipaka kindle agni, digest ama, and relieve kapha-mediated sluggish digestion, making it the principal ingredient of Chitrakadi Vati for weak digestion.

indications.rationale: Arsha (hemorrhoids) in Ayurveda is largely driven by mandagni (weak digestive fire) with vitiated apana vata and kapha. Chitrak's dipana-pachana action corrects the underlying mandagni and its lekhana property helps reduce kapha-meda accumulation; it is the lead herb of Chitraka Haritaki and other arshoghna formulations traditionally indicated for piles. Preliminary in-vitro/animal reports of hypolipidemic and metabolic effects of Plumbago zeylanica/plumbagin; not confirmed by any controlled human weight-management trial
citations.note: No RCT support; tier kept traditional

safety: Hot and sharp (ushna-tikshna) herb that increases pitta. It can cause heartburn, gastric/mouth/throat burning, nausea, or loose stools, especially in pitta-predominant people or those with acidity, peptic ulcer, or inflammatory GI conditions. Start at the lowest dose and use only short-term unless supervised.

CONTRAINDICATED IN PREGNANCY AT ANY DOSE. Plumbago zeylanica root is a uterine stimulant and is traditionally and in folk practice used as an emmenagogue/abortifacient. It must never be taken during pregnancy, and the root must never be used locally or vaginally as an abortifacient (a dangerous folk practice associated with severe injury and death).
Lactation: safety not established; avoid during breastfeeding due to potent plumbagin content and lack of safety data.

The root contains plumbagin, a naphthoquinone that is a direct mucosal and skin irritant and is toxic in excess. Overdose or topical misuse can cause skin blistering/contact dermatitis, severe mucosal irritation, vomiting, diarrhoea, and in large amounts has been associated with serious systemic toxicity (liver/kidney stress and, with deliberate misuse, fatalities). Never exceed low recommended doses; do not apply raw (unprocessed/ashodhita) root to skin or mucous membranes.

Use only properly identified and shodhana (purified/processed) root from a reputable source. Species confusion/adulteration: 'Chitraka' may be supplied as white-flowered Plumbago zeylanica or the more pitta-aggravating, more irritant red-flowered Plumbago indica (P. rosea / Rakta Chitraka); ensure correct identification.

Drug interactions (largely theoretical/preclinical, exercise caution and consult a clinician): possible additive effects with antidiabetic/hypoglycaemic agents (monitor blood glucose); possible effect on platelet function/bleeding risk with antiplatelet/anticoagulant drugs; potential interference with CYP450-metabolised drugs; avoid combining with other hepatotoxic medications.
Avoid in active gastritis, peptic/duodenal ulcer, ulcerative colitis, hyperacidity, and active hemorrhoidal or other GI bleeding (heating, can worsen pitta-type bleeding).
Avoid in infants and young children, and in people with liver or kidney disease, unless specifically directed by a qualified Ayurvedic physician.
Educational information only; not medical advice. Not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease. Use only under the guidance of a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner.

indications.rationale: Through vatanulomana (normalizing downward movement of vata) and relief of distension and spasm, Hingu is traditionally used for gas-associated nausea, belching and a feeling of fullness; its carminative, antispasmodic action on the gut underlies this use.

indications.rationale: Hingu's vatanulomana, shulaprashamana (antispasmodic, relieves colicky abdominal pain) and pachana actions are classically directed at vata-predominant lower-gut complaints with cramping, gas and irregular bowel movement (grahani/udara/colic), which overlap with IBS-type symptoms; snigdha guna offsets the dryness of aggravated vata in the colon. This is a traditional symptom-overlap rationale, not a claim that asafoetida treats IBS as a defined clinical syndrome.

Pharmacological reviews of Ferula assa-foetida describe carminative/antispasmodic activity; however no robust randomized controlled trial supports asafoetida for IBS. Any human IBS data are small, uncontrolled or preliminary and not definitive.
citations.note: No specific journal/year cited and no RCT support found; treat as unproven and verify before publication.

safety: Pregnancy: Avoid in medicinal/therapeutic doses. Asafoetida has reported emmenagogue and possible abortifacient/uterine-stimulant associations in traditional and some pharmacological literature; pregnant individuals should not use it medicinally and should consult a qualified clinician. (requires verification of dose/source)

Lactation: Avoid medicinal/therapeutic doses while breastfeeding. Asafoetida preparations have been linked to methemoglobinemia in young infants; while published cases involved direct infant administration rather than proven transfer through breast milk, supplemental safety in nursing is not established. Ordinary small culinary spice amounts are a separate matter from concentrated supplements.

Infants and young children: DO NOT give asafoetida to infants. There are published case reports of severe to near-lethal methemoglobinemia in infants given Ferula asafoetida (e.g. as 'heltit' or glycerited asafoetida) for colic; infants are especially vulnerable due to immature methemoglobin-reductase activity. Do not use asafoetida-containing gripe-water-type folk remedies in infants.

G6PD deficiency: Use with particular caution (and avoid in infants/children) where glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is known or suspected, given the oxidative/methemoglobinemia risk; a documented near-fatal infant case occurred in a family with G6PD deficiency.

Pitta/heat conditions: Ushna virya and tikshna, katu qualities can aggravate Pitta and may worsen heartburn/acidity (amlapitta), gastritis, mouth ulcers or bleeding tendencies in heat-predominant individuals; use cautiously, ideally fried in ghee.

Drug interactions (preliminary/theoretical - require verification): Asafoetida has reported antiplatelet/anticoagulant (blood-thinning), mild blood-pressure-lowering and possible blood-sugar-lowering effects in in-vitro/animal/preliminary data. Use caution with anticoagulant/antiplatelet drugs (e.g. warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel), antihypertensives, and blood-glucose-lowering medication, and around surgery. These interactions are not well documented in humans; consult a clinician before combining.

Overdose/irritation: Excessive doses can cause burning sensation, belching, gastric irritation, loose stools, headache, or swelling of the lips; very large amounts may cause throat/GI irritation. Stay within small culinary-style amounts.

Adulteration and species/identity risk: Commercial 'hing' is very commonly adulterated or extended with wheat/rice flour, gum arabic, starch, gypsum/chalk, resins or colorants - a quality AND allergen concern (hidden wheat/gluten for sensitive individuals). The botanical source is also taxonomically unsettled: genuine resin may derive from Ferula assa-foetida, Ferula narthex or Ferula foetida (some databases treat 'Ferula asafoetida' as a synonym of F. narthex), so species substitution is common. Source authenticated, food-grade purified resin (shuddha hingu).
Allergy: Can trigger allergic reactions in sensitive individuals; flour-based extenders pose a hidden gluten/wheat allergen risk.

General: Educational information only. Not a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis or treatment. Anyone with a medical condition, on medication, pregnant, breastfeeding, caring for an infant, or planning surgery should consult a qualified healthcare practitioner before use.

indications.rationale: Dhanyaka is classically mutrala (mild diuretic) and trishna-daha-hara. The overnight cold infusion of coriander seeds is a long-standing traditional remedy for daha-yukta mutra (burning micturition) by pacifying pitta in the mutravaha srotas. citations.locator: Animal/in-vitro studies report diuretic and possible nephroprotective activity of Coriandrum sativum seed extracts; only small/uncontrolled human data.
citations.note: Preclinical; supports but does not establish clinical efficacy.

safety: For an EDUCATIONAL wellness app: general information, not medical advice. Coriander is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any condition. Consult a qualified clinician before therapeutic use, especially with an existing medical condition or medication.

Blood sugar / antidiabetic drug interaction: Coriander seed may have a mild blood-glucose-lowering tendency. If you take insulin or oral hypoglycemics, monitor for additive hypoglycemia and consult your clinician before regular medicinal use.

Pregnancy / lactation: Culinary amounts of coriander seed and leaf are customary in food and generally considered safe. Concentrated medicinal/therapeutic doses or extracts during pregnancy and breastfeeding lack adequate safety data and should not be used without professional guidance.

Allergy / cross-reactivity: Coriander is in the Apiaceae (Umbelliferae) family. People allergic to related plants (mugwort, birch pollen, celery, carrot, fennel, anise, dill) may have cross-reactive reactions; occupational and food-allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis, have been reported. Discontinue and seek care if rash, itching, swelling or breathing difficulty occur.

Blood pressure / diuretics: Mild diuretic and possible blood-pressure-lowering effects mean theoretical additive effects with antihypertensives or diuretic drugs; use caution and seek advice if on these medications.

Sedation / CNS depressants: High intake of coriander seed extract has shown CNS-depressant/sedative effects in animal studies; theoretical additive effect with sedatives/CNS depressants at high doses.
Photosensitivity: Some Apiaceae contain photosensitizing furanocoumarins; concentrated/topical contact has rarely been linked to skin photosensitivity in this family.

Adulteration / species confusion: Commercial 'coriander' or its essential oil may be adulterated or confused with other Apiaceae seeds (cumin, caraway, ajwain) or with culantro/sawtooth coriander (Eryngium foetidum) and so-called 'Bolivian/false coriander' (Porophyllum ruderale), which are botanically distinct. Source from reputable suppliers and verify identity. Coriander seed essential oil is highly concentrated and must not be ingested undiluted.
Contaminant note: As with many imported seed spices, watch for heavy-metal or pesticide contamination from unverified sources; prefer tested, food-grade or pharmacopoeial-grade material.

Overdose: Very large therapeutic doses of seed extract have been associated in animal studies with sedation/CNS depression; keep to culinary or recommended educational reference amounts and avoid concentrated extracts without professional guidance.

indications.rationale: Vasa is the classical kasahara (anti-cough) and kaphahara dravya. Its tikta-kashaya rasa with shita virya liquefies and clears vitiated kapha (phlegm) from the respiratory channels (pranavaha srotas) and its chhedana (expectorant) action helps expel sticky mucus, making it a first-line herb for kasa (cough) and pratishyaya (cold) in classical texts. Used here only for short, self-limiting cough and cold.

indications.rationale: Its kapha-pacifying, chhedana (mucolytic) and shvasahara (breathing-easing) properties traditionally support clearing of bronchial passages and comfortable breathing. Modern pharmacology attributes bronchodilatory/expectorant tendencies to the alkaloids vasicine/vasicinone, but this is laboratory/animal-level and combination-product evidence only. This is wellness support for breathing during minor congestion, NOT treatment of asthma, COPD, or active tuberculosis.

Preliminary pharmacology: vasicine/vasicinone reported to have bronchodilatory and expectorant activity in in-vitro and animal studies; human RCT evidence is limited and exists mainly for a fixed multi-herb combination product, not for Vasaka monotherapy
citations.note: In-vitro/animal and combination-product evidence only; not confirmatory clinical proof of monotherapy efficacy for this wellness claim

safety: Educational use only; this is not medical advice and Vasaka is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease. Consult a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner or physician before use.

Pregnancy: Contraindicated. Vasaka and its alkaloid vasicine have documented oxytocic (uterine-stimulant) and abortifacient activity (studied by the WHO as a candidate for fertility regulation); it can stimulate uterine contractions. Traditional use to assist labour does NOT make it safe for self-use, and it must be avoided throughout pregnancy.
Lactation: Insufficient human safety data; avoid during breastfeeding unless specifically directed by a qualified practitioner.
Children: Use only under qualified practitioner supervision; safe self-use doses for young children are not established and the bitter alkaloids may cause GI upset.

Not a substitute for medical care: Do not use to treat or self-manage asthma, COPD, active tuberculosis, pneumonia, or any diagnosed respiratory disease; do not replace prescribed bronchodilators or controller medications. These conditions require a physician.

Drug interactions: Avoid combining with uterotonic / oxytocic drugs (e.g., oxytocin-class agents) due to potential additive uterine stimulation. Theoretical additive effects with cough/expectorant or mucolytic medicines. If you take any prescription medicine, discuss use with a clinician first. (Other interaction claims are not well established.)

Overdose/toxicity: Large or excessive doses may cause nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and gastrointestinal irritation from the bitter quinazoline alkaloids. Stick strictly to traditional/Pharmacopoeial dose ranges and short-term use; long-term and high-dose safety in humans is not well characterized.
Vata caution: Its ruksha (drying) and shita (cooling) qualities may aggravate vata if used in excess or in very dry/depleted individuals; classically combined with honey or demulcents.

Species/identity & adulteration: Often listed under the synonym Adhatoda vasica Nees; ensure correct botanical identity (Justicia adhatoda, family Acanthaceae) and avoid confusion with unrelated 'Malabar nut' look-alikes or adulterated leaf material. Source from a reputable supplier with proper botanical authentication.
Discontinue and seek care if cough/breathing symptoms are severe, persistent (e.g., beyond ~1-2 weeks), worsening, or accompanied by high fever, chest pain, blood in sputum, or shortness of breath.

indications.rationale: Kantakari is a classical kasahara (cough-relieving) dravya. Its tikta-katu rasa, ushna virya, and laghu-ruksha-tikshna gunas liquefy and dry excess kapha obstructing the respiratory passages, while its kaphavatahara action helps clear productive cough and throat congestion. It is a principal herb in Dashamoola and a named ingredient of Kantakari Avaleha indicated traditionally for kasa (cough).

indications.rationale: Beyond simple cough, Kantakari is classically a shwasahara dravya used for breathing wellness. Its tikshna and ushna qualities reduce kapha-mediated bronchial congestion and, by pacifying vata, support smoother movement of prana vata in the chest. It is a component of Kanakasava and Kantakaryadi formulations traditionally directed at bronchial comfort. (Educational respiratory wellness support only; NOT a treatment for asthma, COPD, or any diagnosed respiratory disease.)

citations.locator: Preclinical pharmacology reports describing bronchodilatory, anti-inflammatory and antitussive activity of Solanum xanthocarpum/virginianum extracts in animal/in-vitro models
citations.note: Preclinical/mechanistic evidence only; no controlled human trials (RCTs) confirm efficacy for any respiratory indication.

safety: Educational wellness information only; not a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Consult a qualified Ayurvedic or medical practitioner before use.

Pregnancy: AVOID. Kantakari is traditionally regarded as having emmenagogue/uterine-stimulant potential and contains steroidal glycoalkaloids (solasodine, solasonine, solamargine) of uncertain fetal safety. Note: some secondary sources incorrectly state it is 'safe in pregnancy' - the conservative, safety-first position is to avoid it.
Lactation: AVOID unless directed by a qualified practitioner; safety during breastfeeding is not established and glycoalkaloids may pass into milk.
Infants and young children: contraindicated / avoid. Children are more sensitive to solanine-type glycoalkaloid toxicity.

Glycoalkaloid toxicity: All parts (especially unripe/green berries) contain solanine-type steroidal glycoalkaloids. Overdose or use of green/unprocessed fruit can cause nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, diarrhea, excessive salivation, drowsiness, and in severe cases neurological (confusion, anticholinergic-type symptoms) and cardiac effects. Use only properly processed, classically prepared forms in recommended doses.

CARDIAC CAUTION: Kantakari's glycoalkaloids (e.g., solasodine) have documented cardioactive effects. It should NOT be relied upon as a cardiac tonic ('hridya'), and should be used with caution or avoided in people with arrhythmia, heart disease, or electrolyte disturbances, and in those taking cardiac glycosides (e.g., digoxin), antiarrhythmics, or QT-prolonging drugs. Seek medical advice before use in any cardiac condition.

Pitta aggravation: Its ushna-tikshna nature can aggravate pitta and may worsen acidity, heartburn, gastritis, or bleeding tendencies; best avoided in high-pitta states and in active peptic ulcer disease.

Drug interactions: theoretical additive effects with antidiabetic drugs (possible hypoglycemia - monitor blood glucose), diuretics (additive diuretic/electrolyte effects), antihypertensive/cardiac agents, and CNS depressants (given alkaloid content). Consult a clinician if on these medications.

Reproductive ('vrishya') and cardiac-tonic ('hridya') claims are weakly and inconsistently attested in classical compilations and are NOT supported by reliable evidence; they should not be relied upon as indications.

Species confusion / adulteration: Kantakari (Solanum virginianum, syn. S. xanthocarpum/surattense) is frequently confused or substituted with Brihati (Solanum indicum/anguivi) and other thorny Solanum species, and market samples may be adulterated; source only from a reputable supplier with verified botanical identification.

Discontinue and seek medical care if signs of toxicity occur (persistent nausea, vomiting, dizziness, palpitations, confusion). Do not self-medicate chronic or severe respiratory conditions (asthma, COPD), persistent cough, or any condition requiring medical evaluation.

indications.rationale: Bharangi is a classical kaphahara and shwasahara dravya. Its tikta-katu rasa, ushna virya, and laghu-ruksha-tikshna guna liquefy and clear excess kapha lodged in the pranavaha srotas (respiratory channels), supporting easy, open breathing and clearance of mucus. It is a principal ingredient in classical respiratory decoctions such as Bharangyadi Kashaya. Framed as bronchial/breathing wellness, not as treatment for asthma or COPD.

indications.rationale: By virtue of its tikta-katu rasa and ushna virya with deepana-pachana action, Bharangi digests ama and reduces the kapha accumulation that manifests as productive cough, congestion, and heaviness of the chest. It is traditionally combined with pippali, shunthi and madhu for kaphaja kasa (cough with phlegm) and seasonal congestion.

safety: Educational information only; not a diagnosis, prescription, or treatment. Anyone with a persistent cough, breathing difficulty, wheezing, high fever, blood in sputum, or a chronic condition such as asthma or COPD should consult a qualified healthcare professional rather than self-treating.

Pregnancy: Bharangi has ushna virya (heating potency) and is traditionally regarded as having emmenagogue/uterine-stimulant tendencies; modern reproductive-safety data are lacking. Best avoided in pregnancy except under the direct supervision of a qualified Ayurvedic physician.
Lactation: Safety during breastfeeding has not been established; avoid or use only under professional guidance.
Children and the elderly: use only under qualified supervision and at appropriately reduced doses; the herb is heating and drying.

Constitutional caution (pitta/acidity): being ushna, tikta, katu, tikshna and ruksha, Bharangi can aggravate pitta and may cause dryness, acidity, heartburn, or gastric irritation in sensitive individuals, in high-pitta states, or in those with gastritis/peptic ulcer; reduce or stop if burning, acidity, or dryness occurs.

Drug-interaction caution (theoretical/mechanistic, not quantified in humans): possible additive effects with anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic (blood-sugar-lowering), antihypertensive, and bronchodilator/respiratory medications based on preliminary pharmacological reports; antiplatelet/anticoagulant interaction is not well characterised but caution is reasonable. People on such medications should consult their doctor before use.
Overdose/excess use can cause gastrointestinal upset (nausea, burning, loose stools); stay within traditional dosage and stop if adverse effects appear.

Species confusion and adulteration risk: 'Bharangi' is a controversial source drug in the marketplace. Authentic Bharangi is Rotheca serrata (syn. Clerodendrum serratum), but the root is frequently substituted or adulterated with Premna species (e.g., Premna integrifolia/serratifolia, the Agnimantha group) and occasionally with other Clerodendrum species; mis-identified material can alter potency and safety. Source only correctly authenticated, ideally pharmacopoeially verified, material.
Discontinue and seek medical advice if any allergic reaction, persistent gastric discomfort, or unexpected symptoms occur.

indications.rationale: Tikta-katu, ushna and tikshna; cuts and clears Kapha in the upper respiratory passages and soothes a wet, congested cough - a traditional remedy for colds with chest congestion.

citations.locator: Puṣkaramūla

indications.rationale: Classically described as 'hridya' and the specific herb for parshva-shula (chest/flank discomfort accompanying breathlessness). Modern human evidence is limited to OLD, SMALL, COMBINATION studies - chiefly Inula racemosa together with Commiphora mukul (guggulu) reported as anti-anginal/hypolipidaemic in ischaemic heart disease, plus animal cardioprotection data - NOT robust modern single-herb RCTs. This is therefore only a preliminary, supportive traditional claim and is NOT a treatment for angina, ischaemic heart disease or any cardiac condition; chest pain requires urgent medical evaluation.

citations.locator: use in pārśvaśūla / hṛdroga context

Small/old human combination trials (Inula racemosa + Commiphora mukul/guggulu) for anti-anginal & hypolipidaemic activity and animal cardioprotection studies; weak single-herb evidence
citations.note: No specific journal/author/year asserted; verify against primary literature before any efficacy implication.

safety: NOT a substitute for asthma, COPD, angina or cardiac medication or care - breathlessness and chest pain are RED-FLAG symptoms that need prompt/urgent medical assessment, as they can signal serious heart or lung disease.

Being ushna (heating) and tikshna (sharp) it can aggravate Pitta - may worsen acidity, heartburn, gastritis or peptic ulcer; avoid or use cautiously in Pitta-predominant individuals and on an empty or ulcerated stomach. High or prolonged doses may cause gastric irritation and dryness - keep to recommended amounts and short courses.

Pregnancy and lactation: insufficient safety data. Avoid during pregnancy (warming, sharp, channel-opening herbs are traditionally contraindicated/used with great caution) and while breastfeeding unless specifically advised by a qualified practitioner.
Paediatric: safety in infants and young children is not established - avoid unless directed by a qualified practitioner.

Drug interactions - cardiac: may add to the effects of blood-pressure, anti-anginal, anti-arrhythmic and other heart medicines; use only under medical supervision if you take these. Do not self-treat cardiac symptoms with this herb.

Drug interactions - blood sugar: Inula racemosa has shown hypoglycaemic and hypolipidaemic activity (including in combination with Gymnema and guggulu); it may add to anti-diabetic / glucose-lowering medicines and cause hypoglycaemia. Diabetics should monitor blood glucose and use only with medical guidance.

Species / adulterant risk: 'Pushkarmool' in trade is frequently substituted or adulterated with Saussurea lappa / Saussurea costus (Kushtha) - and Inula racemosa is itself sometimes used to adulterate Kushtha - as well as Inula royleana, Withania, Ricinus (eranda) root and other Inula species. These have DIFFERENT and sometimes more cautious safety profiles: Saussurea costus is CITES Appendix-I listed and its root oil is a known skin sensitiser/allergen. Use only correctly authenticated Inula racemosa root from a reputable, lab-verified source.

Conservation / sourcing: Inula racemosa is a critically endangered Western Himalayan plant; high demand drives adulteration and illegal/wild collection. Insist on authenticated, sustainably and legally sourced material.

General: discontinue and seek medical advice if gastric irritation, allergic reaction (Asteraceae-family sensitivity is possible) or any adverse symptom occurs. This entry is educational and not a substitute for professional diagnosis or treatment.

This use is grounded in classical kaphahara/srotoshodhana action rather than in robust clinical proof.

citations.locator: Small/uncontrolled human trials and animal/preclinical studies of N. sativa seed/oil and thymoquinone report bronchodilatory and airway anti-inflammatory effects; not RCT-grade for general bronchial wellness. Verify against current systematic reviews.

indications.rationale: By reducing kapha and clearing the nasal/upper-respiratory channels (kaphahara, tikshna, srotoshodhana) with shothahara (anti-inflammatory) action, Kalonji is traditionally used to ease sneezing, nasal congestion and itching of seasonal nasal allergy. Several small placebo-controlled trials of N. sativa oil support symptom relief, but the studies are small and heterogeneous, so the evidence is preliminary, not firmly clinical.

citations.note: Krishna Jiraka monograph in the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India lists katu-tikta rasa, ushna virya, kaphavatahara properties consistent with use in kapha-type upper-respiratory and nasal complaints.

citations.locator: Several small randomized/placebo-controlled trials of N. sativa seed oil report reduced nasal congestion, sneezing and itching in allergic rhinitis; small and heterogeneous, so evidence is preliminary. Verify against current systematic reviews.

indications.rationale: Classified as deepana-pachana and balya with krimighna action, Kalonji is traditionally held to clear ama and strengthen agni, which in Ayurvedic terms supports ojas and resilience. Modern support is limited to in-vitro/animal and early-stage human work, so this is a traditional rather than evidence-backed indication.

citations.note: Krishna Jiraka noted as deepana, pachana and balya (strengthening) in classical nighantu tradition.

citations.locator: In-vitro/animal and limited early-stage human studies of N. sativa and thymoquinone report immunomodulatory and antioxidant effects; no robust clinical evidence for immunity/resilience support. Verify against current literature.

safety: Educational use only; not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease. Consult a qualified healthcare professional before use, especially if pregnant, breastfeeding, taking medications, or managing a health condition.

Pregnancy: Avoid medicinal/therapeutic doses. Nigella sativa has shown uterine-stimulant and potential anti-implantation/anti-fertility effects in animal studies; culinary-spice amounts are generally considered acceptable, but concentrated seed extracts or oil at therapeutic doses should be avoided in pregnancy.
Lactation: Traditionally used as a stanyajanana (galactagogue), but high-quality human safety data are limited; use only small culinary amounts unless advised by a clinician.

Diabetes / blood-glucose interaction: N. sativa has a documented blood-glucose-lowering effect and can add to the action of antidiabetic drugs (insulin, sulfonylureas, metformin), risking hypoglycaemia. Diabetics should monitor blood glucose and have medication adjusted by a clinician.

Drug interactions: May add to the effect of antihypertensives (additive blood-pressure lowering) - monitor accordingly. May have additive effects with anticoagulant/antiplatelet drugs (theoretical increased bleeding risk); use caution and consider stopping before surgery. Thymoquinone/seed extracts can modulate cytochrome P450 (e.g., CYP3A4, CYP2C9) and P-glycoprotein in vitro, potentially altering levels of drugs such as warfarin, cyclosporine and statins - separate dosing and seek professional advice; clinical magnitude in humans is uncertain.

Ushna virya, tikshna and pitta-aggravating tendency: in pitta-predominant individuals or in excess it may aggravate pitta/vata, causing heartburn, acidity or gastric irritation; reduce dose or discontinue if this occurs.

Overdose/toxicity: Very high doses of seed oil or isolated thymoquinone have caused gastrointestinal upset, and animal studies show hepatic/renal stress at large doses; do not exceed recommended amounts. Topical seed oil can cause contact dermatitis/allergic skin reactions in sensitive individuals - patch test first.
Allergy: True allergic reactions, including rare reports of anaphylaxis and severe contact/skin reactions, have occurred; discontinue immediately if rash, swelling or breathing difficulty develops.

Species confusion / adulteration: Genuine 'Kalonji' is Nigella sativa, but it is commonly confused with or substituted by onion seed (Allium cepa) and by Cleome viscosa ('wild/false kalonji') seeds, and may be sold mislabelled. Verify botanical identity and buy from reputable suppliers; poorly stored seed can carry aflatoxin and heavy-metal contamination.
Children, pre-surgical patients, and those with bleeding disorders, diabetes or on multiple medications should use only under professional supervision.

Krishna Jiraka (Nigella sativa) monograph in the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India giving rasa-guna-virya-vipaka, karma, parts used and dosage.

karma: Dipana (kindles digestive fire in small doses)
preparation: Component of classical formulations such as Tiktaka Ghrita, Arogyavardhini Vati, and Mahatiktaka Kwatha
typical_dosage: Powder (churna): 0.5-1 g/day in divided doses (some texts cite up to 1-2 g). Decoction: 20-40 ml. Larger single doses are traditionally purgative. Extremely bitter; use the smallest effective amount and only under qualified guidance.

indications.rationale: Kutki is classically tikta-katu with deepana-pachana and pittasaraka (cholagogue) action traditionally directed at the liver (yakrit) and bile, and is a principal ingredient of liver-directed classical formulations (e.g., Arogyavardhini Vati, Tiktaka Ghrita). Its pitta-pacifying bitterness underpins long-standing traditional use for hepatic/bilious support. Phytochemically, the iridoid glycosides picroside I and kutkoside (the 'kutkin' fraction) are associated with hepatoprotective and antioxidant activity in laboratory and animal models.
indications.note: General wellness support for liver/digestive-bile function only — NOT a diagnosis, treatment, or cure for liver disease. Human clinical evidence is limited to small, dated studies; most pharmacological data are in-vitro/animal.

citations.locator: Bhavaprakasha Nighantu, Haritakyadi Varga - Katuka/Katurohini (rasa tikta-katu; kapha-pitta hara; deepana, pachana, bhedana; jvara and yakrit/bile-related use). Virya recorded as ushna in this and the dominant nighantu tradition; a minority of texts cite shita.
citations.note: Classical materia medica for bitter, kapha-pitta-reducing, digestive and bile-supporting actions.

Specific trials, designs, sample sizes, and outcomes must be verified against primary sources.
citations.note: Modern evidence is preliminary and not a robust RCT base; treat as supportive of traditional use only.

indications.rationale: In small doses Kutki acts as deepana-pachana: its tikta-katu rasa, laghu-ruksha guna, ushna virya, and katu vipaka stimulate weak agni and help digest ama. This is the classical basis for low-dose use in sluggish digestion, distinct from its larger-dose purgative (bhedana) action.
indications.note: Low-dose digestive/appetite support only; higher doses are purgative and can aggravate vata.

citations.locator: Bhavaprakasha Nighantu - Katuka: deepana, pachana, krimighna, kapha-pitta hara properties
citations.note: Classical attribution of digestive-kindling and ama-digesting action in appropriate dose.

citations.locator: Charaka Samhita - Katurohini cited among tikta (bitter) dravyas with deepana-pachana / tikta-skandha actions (e.g., bitter group used in jvara and aruchi contexts); exact sutra/chikitsa-sthana locus to be confirmed.
citations.note: Classical bitter-digestives context; verify precise chapter/verse.

safety: For educational wellness use only; this is NOT a diagnosis, treatment, or cure for any disease, including liver disease. Anyone with known or suspected liver disease, jaundice, or abnormal liver tests must consult a qualified clinician before use and should not self-treat.

Pregnancy: AVOID. Kutki is a strong bitter with a purgative (bhedana/rechana) action and an emmenagogue/uterine-stimulant reputation in some traditional sources; safety in pregnancy is not established.
Lactation: AVOID unless directed by a qualified practitioner. Despite classical 'stanya' references, modern safety data in breastfeeding are lacking.
Infants and young children: Do not give to infants; use in young children only under qualified pediatric Ayurvedic supervision, given the bitter cathartic action and risk of fluid/electrolyte loss.

Dose-dependent purgation: At higher doses Kutki is a cathartic and can cause loose stools, abdominal cramping, nausea, and fluid/electrolyte loss; overuse aggravates vata and causes dryness. Use the smallest effective dose for the shortest time.

Hypoglycaemia / antidiabetic drug interaction: Picrorhiza has reported blood-sugar-lowering activity. If combined with insulin or oral antidiabetic agents it may cause additive hypoglycaemia; monitor blood glucose and adjust under medical supervision.

Immunomodulation interaction: Reported immunostimulant/immunomodulatory activity means it should be used cautiously or avoided in people on immunosuppressant therapy (e.g., organ-transplant recipients, autoimmune disease on immunosuppressants); discuss with the prescribing clinician.

Purgative/laxative and electrolyte interactions: Additive effects with laxatives/stimulant cathartics; purgation-related potassium loss warrants caution with digoxin and diuretics. These interactions are largely theoretical/mechanistic and should be confirmed with a clinician.

Hepatic-clearance interactions: As a bitter cholagogue and possible modulator of bile flow and hepatic metabolism, it may theoretically interact with hepatotoxic drugs and drugs cleared by the liver; separate dosing and consult a clinician if on prescription medication.

Hepatotoxicity/overdose caution: Despite its hepatoprotective reputation, excessive or prolonged use is not validated and can cause GI upset; discontinue and seek care if signs of liver upset occur (persistent nausea, dark urine, yellowing of skin/eyes, right-upper-quadrant pain).

SPECIES CONFUSION / TOXIC ADULTERANT RISK: Picrorhiza kurroa is an endangered, over-harvested Himalayan herb and is frequently substituted or adulterated. The trade name 'Kutki' is also applied to the closely related Picrorhiza scrophulariiflora, to Gentiana kurroo, and critically to the TOXIC, unrelated Helleborus niger (which contains cardiotoxic/irritant constituents). Use only botanically authenticated material (ideally standardized to picroside I and kutkoside) to avoid wrong-species and toxic-adulterant exposure.
Allergy/sensitivity: Discontinue if rash, itching, or GI intolerance occurs.
Keep out of reach of children; not a substitute for medical care.

Used traditionally for everyday liver and bile-flow wellness; not a treatment for diagnosed liver disease.

safety: Educational wellness information only; not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease, including liver, kidney, or urinary disorders. Do NOT self-treat hepatitis, obstructive/cholestatic jaundice, or kidney/ureteric stones with this herb - delaying conventional care or attempting to pass an obstructing stone can be dangerous. Consult a qualified healthcare professional first.
Pregnancy: Avoid. Phyllanthus amarus/niruri has traditional and preliminary reports of emmenagogue/uterine activity, and safety in pregnancy is not established.
Lactation: Insufficient safety data; avoid unless advised by a qualified practitioner.
Children: Use only under qualified professional guidance; pediatric safety and dosing are not well established.

Blood sugar: May add to the glucose-lowering effect of antidiabetic drugs and insulin (risk of hypoglycaemia). Monitor blood glucose; use caution around surgery and combine cautiously with other hypoglycaemic herbs (e.g., Gymnema, bitter melon, jamun).

Blood pressure / diuretics: May add to antihypertensive and diuretic effects; combined diuretic use can affect fluid and electrolyte (including potassium) balance and may raise lithium levels - monitor accordingly.
Bleeding / anticoagulants: Possible additive antiplatelet/anticoagulant effect - monitor for bleeding and use caution with warfarin/antiplatelet agents.

Drug-metabolism interactions: May modulate drug-metabolizing enzymes and could alter levels of co-administered medicines, including immunosuppressants and other narrow-therapeutic-index drugs - separate dosing and seek professional advice.

Vata caution: Ruksha (dry) and shita (cooling) qualities may aggravate Vata or cause coldness/dryness if overused or in Vata-predominant individuals; use a warm anupana (e.g., warm water) and avoid overdosing.
Overdose/excess: Excessive or prolonged high doses may cause digestive upset, loose stools, or excessive diuresis; stay within traditional dosage ranges.

Species identity is the principal real-world hazard: genuine Indian Bhumyamalaki is Phyllanthus amarus (P. fraternus is the common substitute). It is frequently confused with or adulterated by P. niruri (the older name, botanically distinct), P. urinaria, and P. debilis, and commercial material is a known source of botanical mix-ups, heavy-metal, pesticide, and microbial contamination. Source only from reputable suppliers with verified botanical identity and quality testing.

Discontinue and seek care if signs of allergy, worsening symptoms, yellowing of skin/eyes, dark urine, abdominal pain, or unusual fatigue occur; do not self-treat suspected liver or kidney disease with this herb.

indications.rationale: Daruharidra is a classical Kushtaghna and Kandughna dravya. Its tikta-kashaya rasa with ushna virya is lekhana (scraping) and kapha-medo-reducing, while the kashaya (astringent) quality is vranashodhana/vranaropana (cleansing and supporting healing of the skin surface). In dravyaguna terms, tikta rasa purifies rakta and reduces the kleda (excess moisture/oozing) and itching associated with mild kapha-pitta type skin imbalances, supporting clearer, healthier-looking skin.

indications.rationale: Daruharidra, particularly as Rasanjana (the aqueous root/stem extract), is one of the most cited classical dravyas for netra-roga and is described as netrya/chakshushya. Its tikta-kashaya rasa and kapha-pitta-reducing action, combined with kashaya (astringent) and shodhana (cleansing) qualities, are traditionally applied as an anjana (collyrium) to support eye comfort in kapha-pitta type ocular irritation (e.g., abhishyanda-type discomfort). This is an established, widespread traditional/classical application and is a TOPICAL preparation requiring sterile, practitioner-prepared material.

indications.rationale: Daruharidra is a classical Pramehaghna and lekhana dravya; its tikta-kashaya rasa, ruksha-laghu guna, and katu vipaka reduce kapha and meda, the doshic factors implicated in prameha (a broad metabolic category). The principal alkaloid berberine has been studied for effects on glucose metabolism, but this evidence is for ISOLATED berberine, not standardized whole Daruharidra; there are no whole-herb RCTs. Framed conservatively as classical/traditional metabolic-wellness support, not a treatment for diabetes.

citations.note: Evidence is for the isolated alkaloid berberine, not whole-herb Daruharidra; downgraded to traditional because whole-herb clinical support is absent. Educational metabolic-wellness framing only, not diabetes treatment. No specific journal/DOI asserted.

safety: Educational information only; not a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease.

Pregnancy: Avoid. Daruharidra/berberine has ushna (heating) and emmenagogue-type potential, berberine can stimulate uterine activity, and berberine is documented to cross the placenta. Standard guidance is to avoid throughout pregnancy.

Lactation, newborns and infants: CONTRAINDICATED. Berberine displaces bilirubin from serum albumin and is associated with risk of kernicterus (severe neonatal jaundice and brain injury) in newborns; it may also pass into breast milk. Do not use while breastfeeding and never administer to neonates or infants.

Pre-existing jaundice / hyperbilirubinemia / G6PD deficiency: Avoid. Because berberine displaces bilirubin from albumin, it can worsen jaundice and is particularly hazardous in those with elevated bilirubin or hemolytic risk.

Drug interactions: Berberine inhibits CYP450 enzymes (including CYP3A4, CYP2D6, CYP2C9) and P-glycoprotein, so it can raise blood levels of many co-administered drugs. Use caution or avoid with: blood-sugar-lowering drugs and insulin (additive hypoglycemia risk), antihypertensives, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, cyclosporine (levels can rise markedly), statins, sedatives, and other narrow-therapeutic-index medications. Separate from medications and consult a clinician/pharmacist.
Hypoglycemia caution: Because of glucose-lowering potential, monitor for signs of low blood sugar if combined with antidiabetic therapy, and avoid stacking with other hypoglycemic herbs.

Dose/overdose: High doses or prolonged use may cause GI upset (nausea, cramping, diarrhea or, conversely, constipation). Its ruksha-ushna nature may aggravate Vata and Pitta in sensitive individuals; avoid in high-Pitta states, bleeding tendencies, or significant dehydration without supervision.

Species confusion / adulteration: 'Daruharidra'/'Tree turmeric' is commercially sourced from several Berberis species (B. aristata, B. asiatica, B. lycium, B. chitria) and is frequently confused or substituted with Coscinium fenestratum (a different genus, IUCN-threatened, often the true 'tree turmeric' in South India) and with Berberis-derived 'Rasaut/Rasanjana.' Curcuma longa (haridra) is a different herb despite the similar name. Use authenticated, correctly identified, heavy-metal-tested material from a reputable supplier.
Topical/ocular (Rasanjana/anjana) use carries contamination and injury risk; eye applications should use only sterile, practitioner-prepared material and not be self-administered.

Use only authenticated material; quality of berberine-based clinical evidence is mixed and largely derived from isolated berberine, not standardized whole Daruharidra. Discontinue and seek care if rash, GI distress, jaundice, or unusual symptoms occur, and consult a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner or physician before use, especially if pregnant, breastfeeding, on medication, or managing a health condition.

indications.rationale: Sariva is a classical raktaprasadana/raktashodhaka (supports healthy blood quality) and varnya (complexion-promoting) dravya. Its madhura-tikta rasa, shita virya and madhura vipaka pacify aggravated pitta and rakta, the doshic factors associated in dravyaguna with heat-driven skin complaints (kushta-type conditions), dullness and blemishes. Its kushtaghna and dahaprashamana karma make it a core ingredient in classical skin-supportive formulations (e.g., Sarivadyasava, Manjisthadi). Framed for everyday mild blemishes and dull skin, not for diagnosing or treating any skin disease.

Pregnancy: AVOID therapeutic/medicinal doses. Some 'sarsaparilla'-type roots are described as emmenagogue/uterine-stimulant and reproductive safety in pregnancy is not established; do not use therapeutic doses in pregnancy.
Lactation: Although classical texts describe Sariva as stanyashodhana (milk-purifying), controlled safety data during breastfeeding are lacking; use only under professional guidance.

Species confusion / adulteration is the MAJOR safety issue for this herb. 'Sariva' is sold as two kinds: Sveta (white) Sariva = Hemidesmus indicus (the canonical drug), and Krishna (dark) Sariva, classically attributed mainly to Ichnocarpus frutescens but frequently substituted in the market with Cryptolepis buchanani. These are botanically distinct species with different constituents and safety profiles (some dark-variety substitutes raise hepatotoxicity/pyrrolizidine-alkaloid concerns). Use only botanically authenticated Hemidesmus indicus root from a reputable, tested source.

Name overlap: the vernacular names Ananta/Anantamula/Anantamool are shared across multiple unrelated drugs, which further increases the risk of receiving the wrong plant; verify botanical identity, not just the common name.

Drug interactions (theoretical/additive): may add to blood-sugar-lowering agents (monitor if combined), antihypertensives, and diuretics (mild diuretic action may add to fluid/electrolyte effects). Caution alongside hepatically metabolized or immunomodulating drugs; space dosing and inform your clinician. No interaction is established by robust clinical data.
Allergy/hypersensitivity: belongs to the Apocynaceae/Asclepiadaceae group; discontinue if rash, itching, or other allergic reaction occurs.

Overdose/toxicity: generally regarded as well tolerated at traditional doses; excessive intake may cause GI upset. Heavy-metal contamination is a known risk for poorly sourced Ayurvedic raw materials - choose products tested for contaminants.
Keep out of reach of children; not a substitute for prescribed medical treatment of skin, urinary, or any other conditions.

safety: Educational wellness information only; not a diagnosis, treatment or cure. Anyone with persistent skin, oral, digestive or other symptoms should consult a qualified healthcare professional.

Pregnancy: Avoid medicinal/therapeutic doses unless supervised by a qualified Ayurvedic physician. Khadira is strongly astringent (kashaya), ruksha (drying) and a classical raktashodhaka; safety of concentrated extracts in pregnancy is not established.
Lactation: Insufficient reliable safety data on therapeutic doses during breastfeeding; avoid concentrated extracts unless professionally supervised.
Infants/young children: Do not give concentrated extracts (kattha) to infants or young children; therapeutic-dose safety in this group is not established.

Vata caution: Ruksha-laghu (dry, light) qualities can aggravate Vata, causing dryness or constipation if overused; balance with suitable anupana (warm water, appropriate fats) and avoid prolonged high-dose use in Vata-predominant or very dry individuals.
Astringent/drying overdose effects: Excess intake may cause constipation, gastric dryness, reduced appetite or, with heavy tannin load, gastrointestinal irritation/nausea.

Hepatic caution: High-dose concentrated catechin/tannin extracts may burden the liver (by analogy with other concentrated catechin extracts); avoid high-dose long-term use, especially with pre-existing liver disease or other hepatotoxic agents/alcohol. Discontinue if signs of liver stress occur. (Requires verification.)

Drug/nutrient interactions: High tannin content can bind and reduce absorption of dietary iron and certain alkaloid/mineral-based and other oral drugs; separate dosing from medications and iron supplements by at least 2 hours. Theoretical additive blood-sugar-lowering effect with antidiabetic agents - monitor blood glucose if using such medication. Theoretical additive astringent effect with anti-diarrhoeal drugs. Catechins may have theoretical antiplatelet/anticoagulant activity - use caution with warfarin, antiplatelets or before surgery.

Species confusion / adulteration: True Khadira is Senegalia catechu (syn. Acacia catechu Willd.). Market 'catechu' may instead be pale catechu/gambir from Uncaria gambir, or Khadira-substitute Senegalia (Acacia) chundra; commercial kattha can be adulterated with starch, clay or unrelated tannin sources. Source pharmacopoeially verified, properly identified material from reputable suppliers.
Quality/contaminant risk: Poorly processed heartwood extracts (kattha) may carry microbial or heavy-metal contamination; choose tested products.
Do not use as a substitute for prescribed medical or dental care; discontinue and seek advice if irritation, allergy, or adverse effects occur.

safety: Educational wellness information only; not a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Consult a qualified practitioner before use, especially with any medical condition or current medication.

Nephrotoxicity / kidney risk: Internal medicinal use (and especially the essential oil) has been associated with kidney irritation, and reports of itching, nausea, GI upset, and blood in the urine (haematuria). CONTRAINDICATED in kidney disease or renal parenchymal disease. Do NOT use internally beyond about 6 weeks; prolonged or high-dose internal use is unsafe.

Essential oil is for EXTERNAL/aromatic use ONLY. Sandalwood essential oil must NOT be ingested for self-use, and must NOT be given internally to infants or children. Never confuse food-grade heartwood powder with concentrated essential oil.

Pregnancy and lactation: Avoid internal/medicinal use during pregnancy and breastfeeding due to insufficient safety data and renal-irritant potential; do not self-administer sandalwood oil internally. Even external essential-oil use should be patch-tested and ideally discussed with a clinician during pregnancy/lactation.

Topical contact dermatitis and photo/allergic skin reactions can occur, especially with the essential oil or fragrance-grade material; sandalwood fragrance components are a recognized contact allergen. Perform a patch test and discontinue if irritation appears.

Drug interactions (theoretical/caution): additive effect with diuretics (increased urinary output) and caution with nephrotoxic drugs given documented kidney-irritation risk at higher internal doses; its ruksha (drying) quality may aggravate Vata-type dryness. Discuss with a clinician if on any chronic medication, particularly renally cleared or nephrotoxic agents.

Species confusion and adulteration are major concerns: true white sandalwood (Santalum album) is frequently substituted/adulterated with Santalum spicatum (Australian sandalwood), 'West Indian sandalwood' (Amyris balsamifera), synthetic santalol fragrances, or oil cut with carrier oils. Critically, RED sandalwood / Rakta Chandana (Pterocarpus santalinus) is a DIFFERENT drug, NOT a substitute for white Chandana. Source only authenticated, lab-verified material; adulterated/synthetic oils raise irritation and allergy risk.
Use cooling, drying Chandana cautiously in those with high Vata, marked dryness, or a cold constitution, as excess may aggravate Vata. Discontinue and seek care if any adverse reaction occurs.

safety: Generally well tolerated as a traditional cooling root infusion or in culinary/sharbat amounts.

Pregnancy: avoid medicinal/concentrated doses and avoid the essential oil entirely. Vetiver essential oil is reported in aromatherapy/review literature to have emmenagogue and uterine-stimulant potential and possible reproductive/developmental toxicity signals; use in pregnancy only under qualified practitioner supervision and avoid concentrated extracts and EO.

Lactation: insufficient reliable safety data for medicinal/concentrated doses and the essential oil; avoid concentrated preparations and EO while breastfeeding unless directed by a qualified practitioner.
Vetiver essential oil is for external/aromatic use only and must NOT be taken internally; do not confuse the aromatic oil with the edible root infusion.
Being cooling, it may not suit those with cold constitutions, weak digestion, or significant Vata derangement when taken in large/prolonged medicinal doses; keep to traditional amounts in such cases.

Drug interactions (theoretical, mainly with high or prolonged doses of concentrated preparations): as a mild diuretic it could add to the effect of diuretic medication and affect fluid/electrolyte balance; possible additive effect with blood-pressure-lowering drugs. Space dosing and consult a professional if on such medicines. These cautions do not apply meaningfully to occasional culinary/sharbat use.

Species/adulterant confusion: authentic Ushira is the root of Chrysopogon zizanioides (older synonym Vetiveria zizanioides). The name 'Khas/Khus' here refers to vetiver root and must NOT be confused with 'khus-khus' poppy seed (Papaver somniferum) nor with other unrelated fragrant grasses; market roots are sometimes adulterated with other aromatic grass roots — source authenticated material.
Use authenticated, contaminant-tested material; aromatic roots may carry heavy-metal or pesticide residues if poorly sourced.

This is educational wellness information only and is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease; consult a qualified practitioner for urinary, skin, or digestive symptoms that persist.

Preclinical studies report bronchodilatory/anti-asthmatic effects of Albizia lebbeck bark extracts in animal models; human evidence limited to small, mostly uncontrolled studies
citations.note: No robust RCT support; tier kept traditional.

safety: Educational information only; not a diagnosis, treatment, or cure. Allergic rhinitis, skin conditions, and breathing problems can be serious - consult a qualified clinician before use, especially if symptoms are severe, persistent, or worsening.

Pregnancy: avoid internal use. Saponins from Cortex Albiziae (related Albizia material) have shown reproductive toxicity in animal studies (marked reduction in pregnancy rate and maternal ovarian/uterine toxicity), and the seeds contain spermatocidal saponin constituents. Do not use in pregnancy except under direct supervision of a qualified practitioner.
Lactation: safety not established in breastfeeding; avoid unless directed by a qualified practitioner.

Fertility: seeds contain saponins with documented spermatocidal activity in studies - seed preparations should be avoided by those seeking conception (both partners) and used only with particular caution.

Anti-diabetic interaction / hypoglycaemia: Albizia lebbeck has shown blood-glucose-lowering activity in animal studies; combined with insulin or oral anti-diabetic drugs it may cause additive hypoglycaemia. Monitor blood glucose and watch for symptoms of low blood sugar.

Other drug interactions: reported effects on bronchial smooth muscle and lipids mean it may theoretically interact with anti-asthma/bronchodilator and lipid-lowering medications. High saponin and tannin content may reduce absorption of co-administered drugs - separate dosing by 2 or more hours and inform your prescriber.

Hepatic and cytotoxicity caution: saponin-rich extracts of Albizia lebbeck show cytotoxic activity against hepatic and other cell lines in vitro; avoid high or prolonged dosing, and use with particular caution in those with liver disease. Discontinue and seek care if jaundice, dark urine, or unexplained fatigue occurs.

Constituent / GI considerations: the bark is rich in tannins (astringent) which in excess can cause GI upset, nausea, or constipation; saponins are potentially mucosal-irritant and hemolytic at high intake. High or prolonged dosing of dry bark may aggravate Vata (ruksha-laghu).
Toxicity / overdose: do not exceed traditional doses. The foliage has caused toxicity in grazing livestock. Discontinue if rash, itching, GI distress, or breathing changes occur.

Species confusion / adulteration: 'Shirisha' may refer to multiple Albizia species - notably Albizia lebbeck versus Albizia procera, Albizia odoratissima, or Samanea saman (rain tree), which are sometimes substituted in trade. Bark of related species is a known adulterant; source only from reputable suppliers with proper botanical authentication.
Children, and people with known plant/legume allergies or on multiple medications, should consult a qualified Ayurvedic or medical practitioner before use.

indications.rationale: In dravyaguna terms Gurmar is tikta-kashaya in rasa, ushna in virya, katu in vipaka and laghu-ruksha in guna, making it a classic kapha-medohara, lekhana (scraping) and deepana-pachana dravya. Excess kapha-medas with weak agni underlie the prameha/madhumeha territory of classical thought, and its bitter-astringent, drying, channel-clearing action is traditionally held to support healthy metabolism. The names Madhunashini/Madhunashana ('sugar destroyer') and the well-known temporary abolition of sweet-taste perception reflect this association. Modern in-vitro, animal and small/uncontrolled human studies report effects on glucose metabolism, but large rigorous RCTs are lacking, so the tier is 'preliminary'. Positioned ONLY for general metabolic wellness, NOT as a treatment for diabetes.
indications.note: No large, well-controlled RCTs establish a therapeutic antidiabetic effect; do not present as diabetes treatment.
